title: Reading Your First Pay Stub
summary: Why your paycheck is smaller than the math you did in your head.
doThisToday: If you've gotten a paycheck before, find your W-4 or your first pay stub and see if you can identify gross pay, withholdings, and net pay.

cards.prompt: You worked 10 hours at $15/hour. That's $150. But your check says $128. What happened to the other $22?
cards.explanation: That's the single most common surprise about first jobs: gross pay (what you earned) and net pay (what actually lands in your account) are two different numbers.

cards.prompt: Gross pay is everything you earned before anything is taken out. Net pay — sometimes called "take-home pay" — is what's left after taxes and other withholdings.
cards.explanation: Every pay stub shows both numbers, plus a breakdown of exactly what got subtracted in between.

cards.prompt: What's typically taken out between gross and net pay?
options: Federal income tax, and FICA taxes (Social Security and Medicare) — state income tax too, depending on where you live
Nothing, gross and net pay are always the same
Just whatever your employer feels like keeping
cards.explanation: These aren't arbitrary — they're required withholdings that fund federal programs. FICA (Social Security + Medicare) applies to almost every paycheck, regardless of how much you make.

cards.prompt: The W-4 is a form you fill out when you're hired, telling your employer how much tax to withhold from each check.
cards.explanation: The W-2 is a completely different form — it's the summary your employer sends you after the year ends, showing everything you earned and everything that was withheld, which you use to file your taxes.

cards.prompt: True or false: the W-4 and W-2 are the same form.
cards.explanation: W-4 = filled out once when you start a job, sets up your withholding. W-2 = sent to you once a year, summarizes what actually happened. Different forms, different timing, different purpose.

cards.prompt: Your gross pay this check is $150. Federal tax, state tax, and FICA together withhold $22. What's your net pay?
cards.explanation: $150 − $22 = $128 net pay — this is the number that actually hits your bank account, and the number you should budget from, not the gross figure.

cards.prompt: Sort each pay stub line item into Gross Pay or Withheld.
cards.explanation: Everything you actually earned adds to gross pay. Everything the government requires your employer to hold back reduces it down to your net pay.

cards.prompt: FICA is actually two separate programs bundled into one deduction.
cards.frontText: What two programs does FICA (the tax on nearly every paycheck) actually fund?
cards.backText: Social Security and Medicare. FICA has stood for roughly 7.65% of your pay for decades — about 6.2% goes to Social Security (funds retirement and disability benefits) and about 1.45% goes to Medicare (funds health coverage for people 65+). Exact rates can shift, so your real pay stub is the source of truth.
cards.explanation: It's not a random fee — it's you paying into the same systems that will eventually support your grandparents, and someday you.

cards.prompt: Rule to remember: always budget using net pay, never gross pay.
cards.explanation: Planning your spending around the bigger gross number is one of the most common first-paycheck mistakes — it guarantees you'll come up short.
